content:
# Luke Chapter 19 — The LOGOS Bible (Formal)
**Translator:** LOGOS | **Source:** Codex Sinaiticus (Aleph, 01), PDF pages 111-113 | **Source Image:** source-texts/greek-NT/LUK_019_page_001.jpg, LUK_019_page_002.jpg, LUK_019_page_003.jpg

---

**1** And having entered, he was passing through Jericho.

**2** And behold, a man called by the name Zacchaeus, and he was a chief tax collector, and he was rich.

**3** And he was seeking to see Jesus, who he is, and was not able on account of the crowd, because he was small in stature.

**4** And having run ahead to the front, he climbed up into a sycamore tree, that he might see him, for he was about to pass through that way.

**5** And when he came to the place, Jesus, having looked up, said to him, "Zacchaeus, make haste and come down, for today I must stay at your house."

**6** And he made haste and came down, and received him rejoicing.

**7** And having seen it, they all were murmuring, saying that he had entered to lodge with a sinful man.

**8** And Zacchaeus, having stood, said to the Lord, "Behold, the half of my possessions, Lord, I give to the poor; and if I have defrauded anyone of anything, I restore it fourfold."

**9** And Jesus said to him, "Today salvation has come to this house, inasmuch as he also is a son of Abraham.

**10** For the Son of Man came to seek and to save the lost."

**11** And as they were hearing these things, having added thereto, he spoke a parable, because he was near to Jerusalem and they supposed that the kingdom of God was about to appear immediately.

**12** He said therefore, "A certain man of noble birth went to a distant country to receive for himself a kingdom and to return.

**13** And having called ten of his own servants, he gave them ten minas and said to them, 'Conduct business until I come.'

**14** But his citizens hated him and sent a delegation after him, saying, 'We do not wish this man to reign over us.'

**15** And it came to pass when he returned, having received the kingdom, that he commanded these servants to whom he had given the money to be called to him, that he might know what they had gained by trading.

**16** And the first came forward, saying, 'Lord, your mina has earned ten minas.'

**17** And he said to him, 'Well done, good servant! Because you were faithful in a very small thing, have authority over ten cities.'

**18** And the second came, saying, 'Your mina, Lord, has made five minas.'

**19** And he said also to this one, 'And you, be over five cities.'

**20** And the other came, saying, 'Lord, behold your mina, which I kept laid away in a cloth.

**21** For I feared you, because you are a severe man; you take up what you did not lay down, and you reap what you did not sow.'

**22** He says to him, 'Out of your own mouth I will judge you, wicked servant. You knew that I am a severe man, taking up what I did not lay down and reaping what I did not sow?

**23** And why did you not give my money to the bank, and I, having come, would have collected it with interest?'

**24** And to those standing by he said, 'Take the mina from him and give it to the one who has the ten minas.'

**25** And they said to him, 'Lord, he has ten minas.'

**26** 'I say to you that to everyone who has, it shall be given; but from the one who does not have, even what he has shall be taken away.

**27** Moreover, those enemies of mine who did not wish me to reign over them — bring them here and slay them before me.'"

**28** And having said these things, he was going on ahead, going up to Jerusalem.

**29** And it came to pass, as he drew near to Bethphage and Bethany, toward the mount that is called Olivet, he sent two of the disciples,

**30** saying, "Go into the village opposite, in which, as you enter, you will find a colt tied, upon which no man has ever sat; having loosed it, bring it.

**31** And if anyone asks you, 'Why are you loosing it?' you shall say thus: 'Because the Lord has need of it.'"

**32** And those who had been sent, having departed, found it just as he had said to them.

**33** And as they were loosing the colt, its owners said to them, "Why are you loosing the colt?"

**34** And they said, "Because the Lord has need of it."

**35** And they brought it to Jesus, and having cast their garments upon the colt, they set Jesus upon it.

**36** And as he was going, they were spreading their garments on the road.

**37** And as he was drawing near, already at the descent of the Mount of Olives, the whole multitude of the disciples began rejoicing to praise God with a loud voice concerning all the mighty works which they had seen,

**38** saying, "Blessed is the King who comes in the name of the Lord! Peace in heaven and glory in the highest!"

**39** And some of the Pharisees from the crowd said to him, "Teacher, rebuke your disciples."

**40** And having answered, he said, "I say to you, if these should be silent, the stones will cry out."

**41** And as he drew near, having seen the city, he wept over it,

**42** saying, "If you had known in this day, even you, the things pertaining to peace — but now they are hidden from your eyes.

**43** For days shall come upon you when your enemies will cast up a rampart against you and will surround you and press you in on every side,

**44** and they will dash you to the ground, and your children within you, and they will not leave in you stone upon stone, because you did not know the time of your visitation."

**45** And having entered into the temple, he began to cast out those who were selling,

**46** saying to them, "It is written, 'And my house shall be a house of prayer,' but you have made it a den of robbers."

**47** And he was teaching daily in the temple. But the chief priests and the scribes and the leaders of the people were seeking to destroy him,

**48** and they were not finding what they might do, for all the people were hanging upon him, listening.

---

## LOGOS Translation Notes

### Manuscript Observations (Codex Sinaiticus)

1. **19:1-2 — "a chief tax collector" (architelōnēs):** Sinaiticus reads ΑΡΧΙΤΕΛΩΝΗΣ. This is a hapax legomenon in the New Testament — the word appears nowhere else in extant Greek literature prior to Luke. It is compounded from ΑΡΧΙ- ("chief, ruling") + ΤΕΛΩΝΗΣ ("tax collector"), denoting a supervisor of tax collectors rather than an individual toll-farmer. Jericho was a major customs station owing to its position on the trade route from the east and its lucrative balsam and date-palm industry. An ΑΡΧΙΤΕΛΩΝΗΣ in Jericho would have managed a network of subordinate collectors, taking a percentage of their collections. The statement ΚΑΙ ΑΥΤΟΣ ΠΛΟΥΣΙΟΣ ("and he was rich") follows naturally — his position guaranteed wealth. Luke's pairing of the title with the adjective ΠΛΟΥΣΙΟΣ sets up the tension with 18:24-25, where Jesus declared the difficulty of the rich entering the kingdom. Zacchaeus is the immediate test case of the assertion in 18:27 that "the things impossible with men are possible with God."

2. **19:3 — "small in stature" (tē hēlikia mikros):** Sinaiticus reads ΤΗ ΗΛΙΚΙΑ ΜΙΚΡΟΣ ΗΝ. The dative ΤΗ ΗΛΙΚΙΑ is a dative of respect — "small with respect to stature." The noun ΗΛΙΚΙΑ can mean either "age" or "stature" (physical height); context here demands "stature," since his inability to see over the crowd is the problem. Luke includes this physical detail with narrative economy: it simultaneously explains why Zacchaeus cannot see (the crowd blocks his view) and why he must take an extraordinary measure (climbing a tree). The verb ΕΖΗΤΕΙ ("he was seeking") in the imperfect indicates persistent, ongoing effort — his desire to see Jesus was not a passing curiosity but a sustained determination. The phrase ΤΙΣΕΣΤΙΝ ("who he is") reveals that Zacchaeus had heard of Jesus but had not yet seen him — his knowledge was by report, and he sought ocular confirmation.

3. **19:4 — "a sycamore tree" (sykomoreian):** Sinaiticus reads ΣΥΚΟΜΟΡΑΙΑΝ (variant of ΣΥΚΟΜΟΡΕΑΝ). The ΣΥΚΟΜΟΡΕΑ (Latin ficus sycomorus) is not the Western sycamore but the fig-mulberry — a tree with a wide, low-branching trunk and broad canopy, common in the Jordan valley. It was easily climbable, with thick limbs near the ground, and its dense foliage would have partially concealed the climber. For a chief tax collector — a man of public standing and presumably of some dignity — to run ahead (ΠΡΟΔΡΑΜΩΝ, from ΠΡΟΤΡΕΧΩ, "to run ahead") and climb a tree was an act of considerable social humiliation. Men of status did not run in public in the ancient Mediterranean world (the father's running in Luke 15:20 is similarly notable), and tree-climbing was a child's activity. Zacchaeus's urgency overrides his dignity, paralleling the desperation of the blind man in 18:39 who cried out despite rebuke.

4. **19:5 — "I must stay at your house" (en tō oikō sou dei me meinai):** Sinaiticus reads ΕΝ ΤΩ ΟΙΚΩ ΣΟΥ ΔΕΙ ΜΕ ΜΕΙΝΑΙ. The verb ΔΕΙ ("it is necessary, one must") introduces divine necessity — the same word used for the passion predictions (9:22, "the Son of Man must suffer"; 13:33, "I must journey on"; 24:7, "the Son of Man must be delivered"). Jesus's self-invitation is not a social pleasantry but a divinely appointed encounter. The imperative ΣΠΕΥΣΑΣ ΚΑΤΑΒΗΘΙ ("make haste, come down") combines urgency (ΣΠΕΥΔΩ, "to hasten") with descent (ΚΑΤΑΒΑΙΝΩ, "to come down") — the same word ΣΠΕΥΔΩ appears in the LXX at Isaiah 49:17, where Jerusalem's children hasten to return. The adverb ΣΗΜΕΡΟΝ ("today") echoes with theological force throughout this episode (vv. 5, 9), linking the moment to the kairos of salvation. Jesus looks up (ΑΝΑΒΛΕΨΑΣ — the same verb used for the blind man receiving sight in 18:42-43) to see the one who had sought to see him: the seeker is found by the one he sought.

5. **19:7 — "they all were murmuring" (pantes diegongyzon):** Sinaiticus reads ΠΑΝΤΕΣ ΔΙΕΓΟΓΓΥΖΟΝ. The verb ΔΙΑΓΟΓΓΥΖΩ ("to murmur throughout, to grumble among themselves") — from ΔΙΑ ("through, among") + ΓΟΓΓΥΖΩ ("to murmur, to grumble") — is an intensified form denoting pervasive, spreading discontent. The same verb appears in Luke 15:2, where the Pharisees and scribes murmur because Jesus "receives sinners and eats with them." The adverb ΠΑΝΤΕΣ ("all") implicates not merely the religious leaders but the entire crowd — the objection to Jesus lodging with a sinner is universal. The phrase ΠΑΡΑ ΑΜΑΡΤΩΛΩ ΑΝΔΡΙ ΕΙΣΗΛΘΕΝ ΚΑΤΑΛΥΣΑΙ ("he entered to lodge with a sinful man") uses ΚΑΤΑΛΥΩ in the sense of "to find lodging, to be a guest" — the same word used in Luke 2:7 for the lodging place where there was no room. The irony is architectural: the one for whom there was no room at his birth now invites himself into the home of one whom society excludes.

6. **19:8 — "if I have defrauded anyone of anything, I restore fourfold" (ei tinos ti esykophantēsa, apodidōmi tetraploun):** Sinaiticus reads ΕΙ ΤΙΝΟΣ ΤΙ ΕΣΥΚΟΦΑΝΤΗΣΑ ΑΠΟΔΙΔΩΜΙ ΤΕΤΡΑΠΛΟΥΝ. The verb ΣΥΚΟΦΑΝΤΕΩ ("to defraud, to extort by false accusation") — etymologically from ΣΥΚΟΝ ("fig") + ΦΑΙΝΩ ("to show"), referring to informers who reported illegal fig exportation from Attica — came to mean any form of extortion through false charge or intimidation. The same verb appeared in Luke 3:14, where John the Baptist warned soldiers: ΜΗ ΣΥΚΟΦΑΝΤΗΣΗΤΕ ("do not extort"). Zacchaeus's use of the conditional ΕΙ ("if") with the aorist ΕΣΥΚΟΦΑΝΤΗΣΑ does not deny past wrongdoing but opens the condition: "if I have defrauded anyone of anything whatsoever." The present tense ΑΠΟΔΙΔΩΜΙ ("I restore, I give back") may be a performative present — "I hereby restore" — or may indicate an already established practice. The ΤΕΤΡΑΠΛΟΥΝ ("fourfold") restitution exceeds the Mosaic requirement: Exodus 22:1 requires fourfold restitution only for stolen sheep (fivefold for oxen), while Leviticus 6:5 and Numbers 5:7 require the principal plus one-fifth. Zacchaeus voluntarily imposes upon himself the most severe penalty in the Torah. The gift of half his possessions to the poor (ΤΑ ΗΜΙΣΙΑ ΜΟΥ ΤΩΝ ΥΠΑΡΧΟΝΤΩΝ ... ΔΙΔΩΜΙ ΤΟΙΣ ΠΤΩΧΟΙΣ) goes beyond any legal requirement — it is the spontaneous generosity of a transformed life.

7. **19:9 — "inasmuch as he also is a son of Abraham" (kathoti kai autos huios Abraam estin):** Sinaiticus reads ΚΑΘΟΤΙ ΚΑΙ ΑΥΤΟΣ ΥΙΟΣ ΑΒΡΑΑΜ ΕΣΤΙΝ. The conjunction ΚΑΘΟΤΙ ("inasmuch as, because") provides the ground for salvation's arrival: Zacchaeus's identity as a son of Abraham. This declaration directly counters the crowd's implicit exclusion of Zacchaeus as a "sinful man" (v. 7). The tax collector, despised as a collaborator with Rome and shunned from the covenant community, is reclaimed by Jesus as a legitimate heir of the Abrahamic promise. The emphatic ΚΑΙ ΑΥΤΟΣ ("he also, even he") insists on inclusion against exclusion. The phrase ΣΗΜΕΡΟΝ ΣΩΤΗΡΙΑ ΤΩ ΟΙΚΩ ΤΟΥΤΩ ΕΓΕΝΕΤΟ ("today salvation has come to this house") uses ΣΩΤΗΡΙΑ ("salvation") — from ΣΩΖΩ ("to save") — as a concrete event that arrives (ΕΓΕΝΕΤΟ, aorist of ΓΙΝΟΜΑΙ) at a specific place (ΤΩ ΟΙΚΩ ΤΟΥΤΩ, "this house") on a specific day (ΣΗΜΕΡΟΝ, "today"). Salvation is not an abstraction but an advent.

8. **19:10 — "to seek and to save the lost" (zētēsai kai sōsai to apolōlos):** Sinaiticus reads ΖΗΤΗΣΑΙ ΚΑΙ ΣΩΣΑΙ ΤΟ ΑΠΟΛΩΛΟΣ. The perfect participle ΑΠΟΛΩΛΟΣ ("the lost one, that which has perished") — from ΑΠΟΛΛΥΜΙ ("to destroy, to lose") — denotes a state of being lost that is already complete and ongoing. The two infinitives ΖΗΤΗΣΑΙ ("to seek") and ΣΩΣΑΙ ("to save") describe sequential actions: first the search, then the rescue. This verse echoes Ezekiel 34:16 (LXX): ΤΟ ΑΠΟΛΩΛΟΣ ΖΗΤΗΣΩ ("I will seek the lost"), where God as the true shepherd promises to do what Israel's false shepherds failed to do. Jesus applies to himself the language that Ezekiel reserved for God — a christological claim embedded in an allusion.

9. **19:11 — "having added thereto, he spoke a parable" (prostheis eipen parabolēn):** Sinaiticus reads ΠΡΟΣΘΕΙΣ ΕΙΠΕΝ ΠΑΡΑΒΟΛΗΝ. The participial construction ΠΡΟΣΘΕΙΣ ("having added") is a Semitism derived from the Hebrew construction וַיֹּסֶף ("and he added," i.e., "he went on to say"). Luke uses this Hebraism to link the parable tightly to the preceding Zacchaeus episode. The reason for the parable is twofold: ΔΙΑ ΤΟ ΕΓΓΥΣ ΕΙΝΑΙ ΙΕΡΟΥΣΑΛΗΜ ΑΥΤΟΝ ("because he was near Jerusalem") and ΚΑΙ ΔΟΚΕΙΝ ΑΥΤΟΥΣ ΟΤΙ ΠΑΡΑΧΡΗΜΑ ΜΕΛΛΕΙ Η ΒΑΣΙΛΕΙΑ ΤΟΥ ΘΕΟΥ ΑΝΑΦΑΙΝΕΣΘΑΙ ("and they supposed that the kingdom of God was about to appear immediately"). The verb ΑΝΑΦΑΙΝΕΣΘΑΙ ("to appear, to be made manifest") — from ΑΝΑ ("up") + ΦΑΙΝΩ ("to show") — means to become visible, to dawn. The disciples and crowds expected the kingdom to materialize upon Jesus's arrival in Jerusalem. The parable corrects this expectation by introducing an interval: the nobleman must go away and return.

10. **19:12 — "a man of noble birth" (anthrōpos tis eugenēs):** Sinaiticus reads ΑΝΘΡΩΠΟΣ ΤΙΣ ΕΥΓΕΝΗΣ. The adjective ΕΥΓΕΝΗΣ ("well-born, noble") — from ΕΥ ("well") + ΓΕΝΟΣ ("birth, race") — designates a man of aristocratic lineage. The historical referent is transparent: Archelaus, son of Herod the Great, traveled to Rome in 4 BC to receive from Augustus the kingship of Judea, while a Jewish delegation of fifty followed to oppose his appointment (Josephus, Ant. 17.299-314; B.J. 2.80-92). The parable maps onto this familiar political event: ΕΠΟΡΕΥΘΗ ΕΙΣ ΧΩΡΑΝ ΜΑΚΡΑΝ ("went to a distant country") parallels the journey to Rome; ΛΑΒΕΙΝ ΕΑΥΤΩ ΒΑΣΙΛΕΙΑΝ ("to receive for himself a kingdom") parallels the petition to Augustus; and the citizens' delegation of opposition (v. 14) parallels the Jewish embassy against Archelaus. Jesus transforms a political memory into an eschatological allegory: he himself is the nobleman who must depart (the ascension), receive the kingdom (the exaltation), and return (the Parousia).

11. **19:13 — "ten minas" (deka mnas):** Sinaiticus reads ΔΕΚΑ ΜΝΑΣ. The ΜΝΑ (mina, Latin mna) was a Greek monetary unit worth 100 drachmas — approximately 100 days' wages for a common laborer. Unlike Matthew's parable of the talents (Matt 25:14-30), where different sums are distributed according to ability, Luke's parable gives each servant an identical amount. This equality of initial endowment shifts the focus entirely to the servants' individual initiative: the varying outcomes cannot be attributed to unequal starting conditions. The command ΠΡΑΓΜΑΤΕΥΣΑΣΘΕ ΕΝ Ω ΕΡΧΟΜΑΙ ("conduct business until I come") uses ΠΡΑΓΜΑΤΕΥΟΜΑΙ ("to be engaged in business, to trade") — from ΠΡΑΓΜΑ ("deed, matter, business affair") — a commercial term denoting active investment. The phrase ΕΝ Ω ΕΡΧΟΜΑΙ ("until I come," literally "in which I come") specifies the period of the master's absence as the time of accountability.

12. **19:14 — "a delegation" (presbeian):** Sinaiticus reads ΠΡΕΣΒΕΙΑΝ. The noun ΠΡΕΣΒΕΙΑ ("embassy, delegation") — from ΠΡΕΣΒΥΣ ("elder, ambassador") — denotes a formal diplomatic mission. The message ΟΥ ΘΕΛΟΜΕΝ ΤΟΥΤΟΝ ΒΑΣΙΛΕΥΣΑΙ ΕΦ ΗΜΑΣ ("we do not wish this man to reign over us") is blunt political rejection. The demonstrative ΤΟΥΤΟΝ ("this man") is contemptuous — they will not even name him. This element has no parallel in Matthew's talent parable and introduces a political dimension absent from a purely economic allegory: the nobleman faces not only unfaithful servants but a hostile citizenry. In the christological reading, the citizens represent Israel's rejection of Jesus as Messiah, and the delegation's mission to the distant country (Rome/heaven) represents the formal opposition to his reign.

13. **19:17 — "have authority over ten cities" (isthi exousian echōn epanō deka poleōn):** Sinaiticus reads ΙΣΘΙ ΕΞΟΥΣΙΑΝ ΕΧΩΝ ΕΠΑΝΩ ΔΕΚΑ ΠΟΛΕΩΝ. The construction ΙΣΘΙ ... ΕΧΩΝ is a periphrastic imperative — literally "be having authority" — denoting an ongoing state of governance. The reward is disproportionate to the task: faithful stewardship of a single mina (a modest sum) results in sovereignty over ten cities. The phrase ΕΝ ΕΛΑΧΙΣΤΩ ΠΙΣΤΟΣ ("faithful in a very small thing") uses the superlative ΕΛΑΧΙΣΤΟΣ ("smallest, least") — the mina is explicitly characterized as a trivial amount. The disproportion between the test and the reward embodies the parable's eschatological logic: the present age is a period of small faithfulness; the age to come will reveal its vast consequences.

14. **19:20-21 — "I kept it laid away in a cloth" (eichon apokeimensn en soudariō):** Sinaiticus reads ΕΙΧΟΝ ΑΠΟΚΕΙΜΕΝΗΝ ΕΝ ΣΟΥΔΑΡΙΩ. The noun ΣΟΥΔΑΡΙΟΝ ("cloth, handkerchief") is a Latin loanword (sudarium, "sweat-cloth"). In John 11:44 and 20:7, the same word denotes the burial face-cloth. The servant's action of wrapping the mina in a cloth and storing it represents minimal preservation without any productive effort — he returns exactly what was given. His justification — ΟΤΙ ΑΝΘΡΩΠΟΣ ΑΥΣΤΗΡΟΣ ΕΙ ("because you are a severe man") — uses ΑΥΣΤΗΡΟΣ ("strict, exacting, harsh") from ΑΥΑΙΝΩ ("to dry") — the word evokes something dry and astringent. The charges ΑΙΡΕΙΣ Ο ΟΥΚ ΕΘΗΚΑΣ ("you take up what you did not lay down") and ΘΕΡΙΖΕΙΣ Ο ΟΥΚ ΕΣΠΕΙΡΑΣ ("you reap what you did not sow") accuse the master of profiting from others' labor — a characterization the master does not deny but turns against the servant (v. 22).

15. **19:22-23 — "Out of your own mouth I will judge you" (ek tou stomatos sou krinō se):** Sinaiticus reads ΕΚ ΤΟΥ ΣΤΟΜΑΤΟΣ ΣΟΥ ΚΡΙΝΩ ΣΕ. The master's judgment operates on the servant's own premises: if you believed me to be severe and exacting, then you should have acted accordingly and at minimum deposited the money with bankers (ΕΠΙ ΤΡΑΠΕΖΑΝ, literally "upon a table" — ΤΡΑΠΕΖΑ being the money-changer's table, whence "bank"). The noun ΤΟΚΟΣ ("interest") — from ΤΙΚΤΩ ("to give birth") — conceives of interest as money that "breeds" money. The argument is a fortiori: even if the servant's assessment of his master's character were correct, his behavior was still inconsistent with it. Fear of the master should have produced, at minimum, cautious investment rather than complete inaction. The servant is condemned not by a standard he could not have known but by the implications of his own stated knowledge.

16. **19:26 — "to everyone who has, it shall be given" (panti tō echonti dothēsetai):** Sinaiticus reads ΠΑΝΤΙ ΤΩ ΕΧΟΝΤΙ ΔΟΘΗΣΕΤΑΙ. This principle appears in nearly identical form in Luke 8:18 and Matthew 13:12; 25:29 — it functions as a general maxim applied to various contexts. The present participle ΕΧΟΝΤΙ ("who has," denoting a current state of possession) is contrasted with ΤΟΥ ΜΗ ΕΧΟΝΤΟΣ ("the one who does not have"). The paradox is deliberate: how can something be taken from one who does not have? The answer lies in the parable's logic — the third servant "had" the mina in his possession but did not "have" it in productive use. Possession without employment is treated as equivalent to non-possession. The divine passive ΔΟΘΗΣΕΤΑΙ ("it shall be given") indicates God as the agent of both bestowal and removal.

17. **19:27 — "slay them before me" (katasphaxate autous emprosthen mou):** Sinaiticus reads ΚΑΤΑΣΦΑΞΑΤΕ ΑΥΤΟΥΣ ΕΜΠΡΟΣΘΕΝ ΜΟΥ. The verb ΚΑΤΑΣΦΑΖΩ ("to slay, to slaughter") — from ΚΑΤΑ (intensifier) + ΣΦΑΖΩ ("to slaughter, to butcher") — denotes violent killing, often of animals for sacrifice. The ΚΑΤΑ prefix intensifies: this is not mere execution but slaughter. The command is given regarding ΤΟΥΣ ΕΧΘΡΟΥΣ ΜΟΥ ΕΚΕΙΝΟΥΣ ("those enemies of mine") — the citizens who rejected his reign (v. 14). Historically, Archelaus did indeed exact violent retribution upon his return from Rome. In the parabolic framework, this verse points to eschatological judgment upon those who reject Christ's kingship. The severity of the image is deliberately shocking — Jesus does not soften the consequences of rejecting the returning king.

18. **19:29 — "Bethphage and Bethany" (Bēthphagē kai Bēthanian):** Sinaiticus reads ΒΗΘΦΑΓΗ ΚΑΙ ΒΗΘΑΝΙΑΝ. Bethphage (Aramaic בֵּית פַּגֵּי, "house of unripe figs") and Bethany (בֵּית עַנְיָה, "house of the poor" or "house of Ananiah") were neighboring villages on the eastern slope of the Mount of Olives, approximately two miles from Jerusalem. Luke names them in reverse geographical order (from Jerusalem outward), suggesting the narrator's perspective is from the city looking east. The Mount of Olives (ΤΟ ΟΡΟΣ ΤΟ ΚΑΛΟΥΜΕΝΟΝ ΕΛΑΙΩΝ, "the mount called Olivet") carried profound eschatological associations: Zechariah 14:4 prophesied that the Lord would stand upon the Mount of Olives in the day of judgment.

19. **19:30 — "a colt tied, upon which no man has ever sat" (pōlon dedemenon, eph' hon oudeis pōpote anthrōpōn ekathisen):** Sinaiticus reads ΠΩΛΟΝ ΔΕΔΕΜΕΝΟΝ ΕΦ ΟΝ ΟΥΔΕΙΣ ΠΩΠΟΤΕ ΑΝΘΡΩΠΩΝ ΕΚΑΘΙΣΕΝ. The noun ΠΩΛΟΣ ("colt, young animal") most commonly denotes a young donkey. The perfect participle ΔΕΔΕΜΕΝΟΝ ("tied, bound") indicates a current state resulting from a prior action — the colt stands waiting. The clause ΟΥΔΕΙΣ ΠΩΠΟΤΕ ΑΝΘΡΩΠΩΝ ΕΚΑΘΙΣΕΝ ("no man has ever sat upon it") specifies the animal as unbroken — never used for ordinary work. In the Old Testament, objects dedicated to sacred use were required to be previously unused (Numbers 19:2, the red heifer; 1 Samuel 6:7, the cows pulling the ark). The unridden colt thus marks the entry as a consecrated act, not mere transportation. The allusion is to Zechariah 9:9: "your king comes to you ... riding upon a donkey, upon a colt, the foal of a donkey."

20. **19:35-36 — "they set Jesus upon it" (epebibasan ton Iēsoun):** Sinaiticus reads ΕΠΕΒΙΒΑΣΑΝ ΤΟΝ ΙΗΣΟΥΝ. The verb ΕΠΙΒΙΒΑΖΩ ("to set upon, to cause to mount") is causative — the disciples physically place Jesus on the colt. The spreading of garments (ΥΠΕΣΤΡΩΝΝΥΟΝ ΤΑ ΙΜΑΤΙΑ ΑΥΤΩΝ, "they were spreading their garments") echoes the enthronement of Jehu in 2 Kings 9:13, where the people spread their garments under him and proclaimed him king. The imperfect tense ΥΠΕΣΤΡΩΝΝΥΟΝ ("they were spreading") and ΠΟΡΕΥΟΜΕΝΟΥ ΔΕ ΑΥΤΟΥ ("and as he was going") create a scene of continuous, processional action — garments were laid down progressively as Jesus advanced. This is a deliberate royal investiture: the crowd is crowning Jesus as king by their actions.

21. **19:37-38 — "Blessed is the King who comes in the name of the Lord" (eulogēmenos ho erchomenos ho basileus en onomati Kyriou):** Sinaiticus reads ΕΥΛΟΓΗΜΕΝΟΣ Ο ΕΡΧΟΜΕΝΟΣ Ο ΒΑΣΙΛΕΥΣ ΕΝ ΟΝΟΜΑΤΙ ΚΥΡΙΟΥ. The acclamation draws from Psalm 118:26 (LXX 117:26): ΕΥΛΟΓΗΜΕΝΟΣ Ο ΕΡΧΟΜΕΝΟΣ ΕΝ ΟΝΟΜΑΤΙ ΚΥΡΙΟΥ ("blessed is the one who comes in the name of the Lord"). Luke alone inserts Ο ΒΑΣΙΛΕΥΣ ("the King") into the psalm quotation — making explicit the royal claim that the other Synoptics leave implicit. The doxology ΕΙΡΗΝΗ ΕΝ ΟΥΡΑΝΩ ΚΑΙ ΔΟΞΑ ΕΝ ΥΨΙΣΤΟΙΣ ("peace in heaven and glory in the highest") echoes the angelic announcement at Jesus's birth (2:14: ΔΟΞΑ ΕΝ ΥΨΙΣΤΟΙΣ ΘΕΩ ΚΑΙ ΕΠΙ ΓΗΣ ΕΙΡΗΝΗ, "glory in the highest to God and on earth peace"). The chiastic reversal is significant: at the nativity, glory was in heaven and peace on earth; at the triumphal entry, peace is in heaven and glory in the highest. The shift suggests that the peace Jesus brings operates first in the heavenly realm — the cosmic reconciliation — before its earthly manifestation. The whole multitude of the disciples (ΑΠΑΝ ΤΟ ΠΛΗΘΟΣ ΤΩΝ ΜΑΘΗΤΩΝ) — Luke specifies the acclamation comes from disciples, not the general crowd.

22. **19:39-40 — "the stones will cry out" (hoi lithoi kraxousin):** Sinaiticus reads ΟΙ ΛΙΘΟΙ ΚΡΑΞΟΥΣΙΝ. The Pharisees' demand ΕΠΙΤΙΜΗΣΟΝ ΤΟΙΣ ΜΑΘΗΤΑΙΣ ΣΟΥ ("rebuke your disciples") seeks to suppress the messianic acclamation. Jesus's response is a conditional statement with a striking apodosis: ΕΑΝ ΟΥΤΟΙ ΣΙΩΠΗΣΟΥΣΙΝ ΟΙ ΛΙΘΟΙ ΚΡΑΞΟΥΣΙΝ ("if these should be silent, the stones will cry out"). The verb ΚΡΑΖΩ ("to cry out") applied to stones is a powerful personification — creation itself will testify if human testimony is suppressed. The image may allude to Habakkuk 2:11 (LXX): ΛΙΘΟΣ ΕΚ ΤΟΙΧΟΥ ΒΟΗΣΕΤΑΙ ("a stone from the wall will cry out"), where stones bear witness against injustice. The theological implication is that the acclamation is not optional but cosmically necessary — the truth of Jesus's kingship demands expression, and if rational beings refuse it, inanimate creation will supply it.

23. **19:41-42 — "he wept over it" (eklausen ep' autē):** Sinaiticus reads ΕΚΛΑΥΣΕΝ ΕΠ ΑΥΤΗ. The verb ΚΛΑΙΩ ("to weep, to wail") denotes audible weeping — not silent tears (ΔΑΚΡΥΩ) but vocal lamentation. This is one of only two occasions in the Gospels where Jesus weeps (cf. John 11:35, where ΕΔΑΚΡΥΣΕΝ is used — a quieter term). The object of his grief is the city (ΤΗΝ ΠΟΛΙΝ) — Jerusalem as a whole. The conditional sentence ΕΙ ΕΓΝΩΣ ΕΝ ΤΗ ΗΜΕΡΑ ΤΑΥΤΗ ΚΑΙ ΣΥ ΤΑ ΠΡΟΣ ΕΙΡΗΝΗΝ ("if you had known in this day, even you, the things pertaining to peace") is an unfulfilled condition — the protasis is stated but the apodosis is suppressed, trailing off into the devastating NUN ΔΕ ΕΚΡΥΒΗ ΑΠΟ ΟΦΘΑΛΜΩΝ ΣΟΥ ("but now they are hidden from your eyes"). The divine passive ΕΚΡΥΒΗ ("they were hidden") implies that God has concealed from Jerusalem the very peace that stood before her in the person of Jesus. The emphatic ΚΑΙ ΣΥ ("even you") carries a note of tragic irony: Jerusalem, whose very name contains the word ΕΙΡΗΝΗ/שָׁלוֹם ("peace"), does not recognize the things that belong to her peace.

24. **19:43-44 — "they will not leave in you stone upon stone" (ouk aphēsousin lithon epi lithon en soi):** Sinaiticus reads ΟΥΚ ΑΦΗΣΟΥΣΙΝ ΛΙΘΟΝ ΕΠΙ ΛΙΘΟΝ ΕΝ ΣΟΙ. The prophecy describes four stages of Jerusalem's destruction: (1) ΠΑΡΕΜΒΑΛΟΥΣΙΝ ΟΙ ΕΧΘΡΟΙ ΣΟΥ ΧΑΡΑΚΑ ΣΟΙ ("your enemies will cast up a rampart against you") — ΧΑΡΑΞ denotes a palisade or siege-work, from ΧΑΡΑΣΣΩ ("to sharpen, to stake"); (2) ΠΕΡΙΚΥΚΛΩΣΟΥΣΙΝ ΣΕ ("they will surround you") — complete encirclement; (3) ΣΥΝΕΞΟΥΣΙΝ ΣΕ ΠΑΝΤΟΘΕΝ ("they will press you in on every side") — ΣΥΝΕΧΩ means "to press together, to compress," from ΣΥΝ ("together") + ΕΧΩ ("to hold"); (4) ΕΔΑΦΙΟΥΣΙΝ ΣΕ ΚΑΙ ΤΑ ΤΕΚΝΑ ΣΟΥ ΕΝ ΣΟΙ ("they will dash you to the ground, and your children within you") — ΕΔΑΦΙΖΩ means "to level to the ground," from ΕΔΑΦΟΣ ("ground, bottom"). Each stage was fulfilled precisely in Titus's siege of AD 70: the Roman circumvallation wall, the encirclement that produced famine, the assault, and the utter destruction of the temple. The reason clause ΑΝΘ ΩΝ ΟΥΚ ΕΓΝΩΣ ΤΟΝ ΚΑΙΡΟΝ ΤΗΣ ΕΠΙΣΚΟΠΗΣ ΣΟΥ ("because you did not know the time of your visitation") uses ΕΠΙΣΚΟΠΗ ("visitation, inspection") — from ΕΠΙ ("upon") + ΣΚΟΠΕΩ ("to look, to inspect") — denoting a divine visit for the purpose of examination and potential blessing. The same word appears in 1 Peter 2:12 for the "day of visitation." Jerusalem's destruction is attributed not to Roman military superiority but to a failure of spiritual recognition.

25. **19:45-46 — "a den of robbers" (spēlaion lēstōn):** Sinaiticus reads ΣΠΗΛΑΙΟΝ ΛΗΣΤΩΝ. Jesus's temple action combines two prophetic texts: Isaiah 56:7 (ΚΑΙ Ο ΟΙΚΟΣ ΜΟΥ ΟΙΚΟΣ ΠΡΟΣΕΥΧΗΣ, "and my house shall be a house of prayer") and Jeremiah 7:11 (ΜΗ ΣΠΗΛΑΙΟΝ ΛΗΣΤΩΝ Ο ΟΙΚΟΣ ΜΟΥ, "has my house become a den of robbers?"). Luke's account is notably compressed compared to Mark's — he omits the overturning of tables and seats, reducing the action to ΗΡΞΑΤΟ ΕΚΒΑΛΛΕΙΝ ΤΟΥΣ ΠΩΛΟΥΝΤΑΣ ("he began to cast out those who were selling"). The verb ΕΚΒΑΛΛΩ ("to cast out, to drive out") is the same word used for the exorcism of demons — the expulsion of commerce from the temple is narrated with the vocabulary of spiritual cleansing. The noun ΛΗΣΤΗΣ ("robber, bandit") denotes not a petty thief (ΚΛΕΠΤΗΣ) but a violent brigand — Josephus uses the word for revolutionary insurgents. Jeremiah's original context (7:1-15) warned that the temple would be destroyed if it became a refuge for those who practiced injustice — a warning that connects directly to Jesus's prophecy of the temple's destruction in verses 43-44.

26. **19:47-48 — "hanging upon him, listening" (exekremato autou akouōn):** Sinaiticus reads ΕΞΕΚΡΕΜΑΤΟ ΑΥΤΟΥ ΑΚΟΥΩΝ. The verb ΕΚΚΡΕΜΑΜΑΙ ("to hang upon, to hang on the words of") — from ΕΚ ("from") + ΚΡΕΜΑΜΑΙ ("to hang") — is a vivid image: the people are literally "suspended from him" in rapt attention. The word appears only here in the New Testament. This popular devotion creates a political stalemate: the chief priests (ΑΡΧΙΕΡΕΙΣ), scribes (ΓΡΑΜΜΑΤΕΙΣ), and leaders of the people (ΠΡΩΤΟΙ ΤΟΥ ΛΑΟΥ) seek (ΕΖΗΤΟΥΝ, imperfect — ongoing, persistent effort) to destroy him (ΑΠΟΛΕΣΑΙ), but ΟΥΧ ΕΥΡΙΣΚΟΝ ΤΟ ΤΙ ΠΟΙΗΣΩΣΙΝ ("they were not finding what they might do"). The deliberative subjunctive ΠΟΙΗΣΩΣΙΝ ("they might do") reveals their frustrated deliberation — they want to act but cannot find a way. The people's attachment to Jesus is simultaneously his protection and the authorities' problem. Luke thus closes the chapter at a point of maximum tension: Jesus has claimed the temple, the crowds are captivated, and the authorities are plotting — the stage is set for the passion.

### Translation Philosophy

1. **Formal equivalence and the Zacchaeus narrative:** The Zacchaeus episode (vv. 1-10) is one of Luke's most narratively vivid passages, rich with participles, compound verbs, and rapid scene changes. The formal translation preserves the participial chains (ΠΡΟΔΡΑΜΩΝ ... ΑΝΕΒΗ, "having run ahead ... he climbed up"; ΣΠΕΥΣΑΣ ΚΑΤΑΒΗΘΙ, "having made haste, come down"; ΣΤΑΘΕΙΣ ... ΕΙΠΕΝ, "having stood ... said") that create the episode's characteristic momentum. Each participle marks a discrete physical action, and the formal rendering maintains the sequence rather than condensing it into smoother English.

2. **Preserving the mina parable's political framework:** Luke's parable of the minas (vv. 11-27) differs from Matthew's parable of the talents not merely in monetary amounts but in structural complexity — it includes the political subplot of the nobleman's contested kingship (vv. 12, 14, 27) that is entirely absent from Matthew. The formal translation preserves this dual structure without harmonizing with Matthew, rendering the parable as Luke received it from his sources and as Sinaiticus transmits it.

3. **The lament over Jerusalem as incomplete conditional:** The formal translation preserves the aposiopesis (breaking-off) in verse 42: "If you had known in this day, even you, the things pertaining to peace — but now they are hidden from your eyes." The dash marks the point where the sentence collapses into grief, the apodosis left forever unspoken. A dynamic translation might complete the thought; the formal translation follows the Greek syntax, which refuses completion.

4. **Continuity with Luke 18:** The chapter opens with a scene that directly tests the theological assertions of chapter 18. The rich ruler could not part with his wealth (18:23); Zacchaeus spontaneously gives away half of his possessions (19:8). Jesus declared it impossible for the rich to enter the kingdom (18:24-25) but possible with God (18:27); Zacchaeus is the living demonstration. The formal translation preserves the verbal echoes — ΠΛΟΥΣΙΟΣ ("rich") in both 18:23 and 19:2; ΣΩΤΗΡΙΑ/ΣΩΖΩ ("salvation/to save") in 19:9-10 answering the question of 18:26 ("who is able to be saved?") — allowing the reader to trace Luke's narrative theology across the chapter boundary.

5. **Temple as literary and theological culmination:** Luke 19 moves Jesus from Jericho (the lowest city) to Jerusalem (the holy city) to the temple (the holiest place), a geographic and theological ascent that culminates in the cleansing of the temple and the establishment of Jesus's daily teaching ministry there (v. 47). The formal translation preserves the geographic markers (ΙΕΡΙΧΩ, ΒΗΘΦΑΓΗ, ΒΗΘΑΝΙΑ, ΤΟ ΟΡΟΣ ΤΟ ΚΑΛΟΥΜΕΝΟΝ ΕΛΑΙΩΝ, ΙΕΡΟΥΣΑΛΗΜ, ΤΟ ΙΕΡΟΝ) that trace this ascent, rendering each place name consistently and marking the spatial progression that structures the chapter.
